did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning techniques, specifically a 2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a Residual Neural Network (ResNet), and a Self-Organizing Map Neural Network (SOM), to classify melanoma from dermoscopic images in the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing three neural network approaches (2D CNN, ResNet, SOM) for melanoma classification using the ISIC dataset. It includes specific methodology, pre-processing steps, training/validation/testing procedures, and quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC), which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: A Comparison of Neural Network Approaches for Melanoma Classification
brief_summary: This study compares three deep learning approaches—2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Residual Neural Network (ResNet), and Self-Organizing Map (SOM)—for classifying melanoma using the HAM10000 dataset from ISIC. The methodology involved a specific pre-processing phase including hair removal and data augmentation to address class imbalance, followed by binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics and compared against dermatologist performance. The ResNet model achieved the highest accuracy of 81.5%, while the 2D CNN demonstrated superior sensitivity at 89.4%. The neural networks generally outperformed dermatologists in terms of accuracy and specificity, with the ResNet achieving an AUC of 0.875.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). In the HAM10000 dataset, this class contained 1,113 images.

All other classes in the HAM10000 dataset were grouped into a single 'not mel' class for the binary classification experiment. This included melanocytic nevi, benign keratoses, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions.

paper_terms: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) cases using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (2D CNN, ResNet, SOM) predict discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. not melanoma). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions from the ISIC dataset. The study used this dataset for binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma.
classes_used: not mel (non-melanoma, comprising akiec, bcc, bkl, df, nv, vasc)

use.training: Used to train the 2D CNN, ResNet, and SOM models after binarization, hair removal, and data augmentation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the trained models (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC).
use.other: The dataset was pruned by removing ~10% of images with occlusions. The problem was binarized into 'mel' and 'not mel' classes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the goodness of the adopted neural networks and compare them with manual classification of dermatologists.
To evaluate the diagnostic classification performance of the best model (ResNet) and analyze the compromise between sensitivity and specificity.
To compare the performance of neural networks against human experts (dermatologists) in terms of sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between neural networks and dermatologists were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (ISIC).split: Training/Validation/Test (percentages not explicitly stated, but same split used for all models)
HAM10000 (ISIC).notes: The ResNet model achieved the highest accuracy (81.5%) and specificity (79%). The 2D CNN demonstrated superior sensitivity (89.4%). All neural networks outperformed dermatologists in terms of specificity. The pre-processing phase improved accuracy by 2.5% for ResNet, 2.2% for 2D CNN, and 2.2% for SOM compared to results without pre-processing. Without pre-processing, ResNet had 79% accuracy, 2D CNN had 71.9% accuracy, and SOM had 66.8% accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Three models were compared: 2D Convolutional Neural Network (2D CNN), Residual Neural Network (ResNet), and Self-Organizing Map (SOM, specifically SUSI variant).
architecture_used.input_size: 300 x 225 pixels (resized from original 600 x 450 pixels)

2D CNN.structure: 4 convolutional blocks (composed of Convolutional 2D and Maxpooling layers) and one fully connected (FC) block.
2D CNN.activation_functions: Rectified Linear Units (ReLUs) for convolutional levels; Sigmoid for the last FC level.
2D CNN.regularization: L2 kernel regularization for convolutional and dense layers.
2D CNN.dropout: Two levels of Dropout added; one after all convolutional levels.

ResNet.structure: Residual Neural Network utilizing residual learning to avoid gradient cancellation.
ResNet.details: Specific layer counts or block configurations were not explicitly detailed in the text, but it was identified as the best performing model.

SOM (SUSI).type: Self-Organizing Map combining unsupervised and supervised learning.

training_details.data_split: 80% training, 20% test; 20% of the training set used for validation.
training_details.preprocessing: Hair removal, median filtering for over-segmentation reduction, and Data Augmentation to handle class imbalance.

preprocessing_techniques: Pruning of images with occlusions (~10% removed)
Hair removal using Canny edge detection, Otsu's thresholding for mask generation, dilation operator for precision, and image inpainting for hair repair
Lesion segmentation using Otsu's Thresholding to separate lesion from background
Data Augmentation to handle class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Concatenated operations applied to original melanoma images to generate seven distinct images per original: horizontal flips, vertical flips, and rotations of 180, 90, and -90 degrees. This increased the melanoma class size from 1,113 to 8,904 images.
Images were randomly augmented during the training execution at each iteration, allowing the training algorithm to learn from augmented images dynamically.
Combined with real-time augmentation to handle unbalanced data. Used a heuristic approach inspired by King et al. [25] for weight balancing.

conclusions: The ResNet model was identified as the most effective neural network approach for melanoma classification in terms of accuracy (81.5%) and specificity (79%).
The 2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) demonstrated superior sensitivity (89.4%) compared to the other models.
The Self-Organizing Map (SOM) model achieved the lowest performance among the tested architectures, with an accuracy of 69%.
The pre-processing phase (including hair removal and data augmentation) improved classification accuracy by 2.2% to 2.5% across all neural network models.
All tested neural networks outperformed dermatologists in terms of specificity, while the 2D CNN and ResNet showed greater sensitivity than the dermatologist baseline.
The ResNet model achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.875, indicating good discrimination capability.
The study acknowledges that despite the high performance of AI models, they do not claim to replace human experts, emphasizing the fundamental importance of the dermatologist's role.
The static nature and fixed topological structure of the SOM grid were identified as limitations, leading to poor data distribution and inferior performance compared to CNNs and ResNets.
The ResNet architecture mitigated the gradient cancellation problem often found in standard 2D CNNs through residual learning, contributing to its higher accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (1,113 melanoma vs. 8,902 non-melanoma images) and multi-source bias, potentially limiting generalizability.

The problem was binarized into 'melanoma' vs. 'non-melanoma', grouping six distinct benign and malignant non-melanoma classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, nv, vasc) together, which may obscure class-specific diagnostic challenges.

Data augmentation was applied exclusively to the melanoma class (increasing it from 1,113 to 8,904 images) to handle imbalance, which may introduce synthetic bias or overfitting risks for the minority class.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between models or against dermatologists.

The comparison with dermatologists used aggregated mean scores from external literature (references [36] and another ISIC study) rather than a concurrent, controlled clinical trial, limiting the validity of the human-AI comparison.
The ResNet model achieved an accuracy of 81.5%, which is only marginally higher than the reported dermatologist accuracy of 80%, suggesting limited clinical superiority in absolute terms.
The Self-Organizing Map (SOM) model performed poorly (69% accuracy) compared to CNNs, attributed to its static topological structure and inability to capture complex spatial features effectively.
Pre-processing steps (hair removal, resizing, augmentation) improved accuracy by 2.2-2.5%, highlighting the critical dependency of these models on specific, non-standardized pre-processing pipelines.
The dataset was pruned by removing approximately 10% of images with occlusions, which may introduce selection bias by excluding difficult or ambiguous cases.
The study does not report external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about the robustness and generalizability of the findings to diverse clinical populations.
